title: Forcepoint DLP Incident Escalation and Severity Progression (`forcepoint:dlp`)
dataSources: Forcepoint cloud SSE (`forcepoint:one`), on-prem Web Security (`websense:cg:kv`), Email (`forcepoint:email:kv`), DLP (`forcepoint:dlp`)
app: Forcepoint Insights SIEM App (Splunkbase 8053); Forcepoint Web Security TA (`websense:cg:kv`); Email Security KV (`forcepoint:email:kv`)
spl:
index=dlp sourcetype="forcepoint:dlp" earliest=-30d
| stats earliest(severity) as start_sev latest(severity) as end_sev by incident_id
| where start_sev!=end_sev
| sort incident_id
description: Escalations indicate repeat offenders or growing data exposure.
value: Data protection managers track how DLP incidents escalate through severity levels and workflow stages, measuring whether initial classification is accurate and identifying incidents that require legal or HR intervention.
implementation: Install the Insights SIEM app, configure HEC inputs per product line, and align indexes (`proxy`, `email`, `dlp`, `sase`). Normalize usernames to enterprise SAMAccountName.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Forcepoint Web Security

knownFalsePositives: DLP hits from authorized data exports, finance reports, contracts with PII content sent to vendors.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Forcepoint DLP Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 6507) or syslog integration from Forcepoint DLP.
• Data in `index=dlp` with `sourcetype=forcepoint:dlp`. Key fields: `incident_id`, `severity` (high/medium/low), `status` (new/escalated/investigating/resolved/closed), `escalated_to`, `escalation_reason`, `user`, `data_pattern`, `channel`, `policy_name`, `timestamp`.
• Forcepoint DLP with incident escalation workflow configured — incidents can be escalated from analyst to manager, legal, or HR based on severity and data type.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify DLP incident escalation events:
```spl
index=dlp sourcetype="forcepoint:dlp" earliest=-30d
| where isnotnull(incident_id)
| stats count values(status) as statuses values(severity) as severities by incident_id
| where mvcount(statuses) > 1
| head 10
```
Incidents with multiple status values have gone through state transitions.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Severity progression analysis:
```spl
index=dlp sourcetype="forcepoint:dlp" earliest=-30d
| sort incident_id, _time
| stats earliest(severity) as initial_severity latest(severity) as current_severity count as updates values(escalated_to) as escalation_path values(channel) as channels by incident_id, user, policy_name
| eval escalated=if(initial_severity!=current_severity, "YES", "NO")
| eval direction=case(
    initial_severity="low" AND current_severity IN ("medium", "high"), "ESCALATED",
    initial_severity="medium" AND current_severity="high", "ESCALATED",
    initial_severity="high" AND current_severity IN ("medium", "low"), "DOWNGRADED",
    1==1, "UNCHANGED"
)
| stats count by direction, initial_severity, current_severity
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL: Tracks how DLP incidents change severity over their lifecycle. High escalation rates (low-to-high) indicate initial severity classification is too conservative — the DLP system needs tuning. Frequent downgrades (high-to-low) suggest false positive issues. Incidents that escalate to legal or HR represent the most serious data loss cases.

Escalation path analysis:
```spl
index=dlp sourcetype="forcepoint:dlp" earliest=-30d
| where isnotnull(escalated_to)
| stats count dc(incident_id) as incidents by escalated_to, policy_name
| sort - incidents
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In Forcepoint Security Manager: Data Security > Incidents > filter by Escalated. Compare with Splunk.
(b) Escalate a test incident and verify the state change appears in Splunk.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Forcepoint — DLP Escalations"):
• Row 1 — Single-value: "Escalated incidents (30d)", "Escalation rate (%)", "Avg escalation time".
• Row 2 — Sankey or flow diagram: initial severity > current severity.
• Row 3 — Table: escalated incidents with user, policy, initial/current severity, escalation path.

Alert: Immediate alert when any incident is escalated to Legal or HR. Weekly escalation summary.

Runbook:
1. **High escalation rate** — Tune DLP initial severity classification. If most 'low' incidents escalate, the threshold is too low.
2. **Incident escalated to Legal** — Potential intentional data theft. Preserve evidence. Follow the legal hold process.
3. **Same user repeatedly escalated** — Pattern of behavior. Consider HR intervention or access restrictions.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No escalation data** — Escalation workflow may not be configured in Forcepoint DLP.
• **Severity never changes** — Incidents may be resolved without proper state transitions. Train analysts.
• **`escalated_to` empty** — Escalation path may not be logged. Check DLP audit settings.
grandmaExplanation: We track how data-loss incidents get passed up the chain from small to serious, so we can see if our initial ratings are right and catch cases that need legal attention.
